description: A summer, discovery, or bridge is 'waora' when it exceeds what the experiencer expected and awakens wonder. The response may arise from an ordinary event newly noticed. 'wakomi' (surprise) is the unexpected arrival, while 'mioru' (beautiful) is an aesthetic judgement; either quality can occur without wonder.
articulatory_notes: Round the lips and raise the back of the tongue for voiced 'w', then open into central unrounded 'a'. Raise the back of the tongue and round the lips for a separate stressed true-mid 'o', preserving the 'a.o' hiatus. Make a tap or short trill with the tongue tip for 'r' and finish with open central 'a'.
sound_symbolism: 'waora' begins with the full sound of 'wao' (wow), the response most likely to accompany it. The added tongue tap and final open vowel let the exclamation continue as a description.
usage_notes: 'wao' is the response; 'waora' is the quality that prompts it. Noun position yields wondrousness. Felt wonder can be stated as a response, while wonder about a question keeps that question inside 'phaelo' (feel).

pillars.secular-buddhist-philosophy: An ordinary morning may become wondrous when settled expectation yields to attention.

semantic_domains.cognition: an experience judged to exceed the expectation brought to it
semantic_domains.emotion: a person, event, or thing that prompts a felt response of wonder
